title: French praline
subtitle: Caramelised almonds and hazelnuts, ground to a paste or left as brittle
excerpt: French praline is made by cooking whole nuts in sugar syrup until the sugar caramelises and coats the nuts, then pouring the mixture out to set. Ground to a powder, it becomes praline powder used in buttercreams and desserts. Left in shards, it is praline brittle. Both start from the same process: amber caramel and toasted nuts.
sourceNotes: Praline is a French confectionery preparation, attributed by tradition to the household of César de Choiseul, comte du Plessis-Praslin, in the seventeenth century. The formula is firmly public domain: sugar cooked to amber caramel with nuts stirred in. French patisserie uses praline paste extensively; the brittle form is also an eating confection.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze ground praline in an airtight container. Use from frozen.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly in a larger pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 weeks, or indefinitely frozen.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: amber caramel stage, approximately 165°C; the caramel should be deep copper, not pale gold

content.text: Praline is nuts coated in amber caramel, poured onto an oiled surface and left to harden into a sheet. The sheet can be eaten as a brittle, broken into shards for decoration, or ground in a food processor to a powder or paste depending on how long you run it. Ground praline paste stirred into buttercream is the classic filling for Paris-Brest and other pastries.
Sugar at caramel stage, around 165°C, causes serious burns. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.
Spread the nuts on a baking tray and toast in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes until lightly golden. Set aside; warm nuts are easier to coat than cold.

Combine the sugar and water in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Do not stir once the sugar has dissolved; swirl the pan instead if needed. Cook until the syrup turns deep amber; 165°C on a sugar thermometer. At this stage the caramel smells of toffee and the colour is a rich copper. Work quickly from here; it continues to darken off the heat.

Remove from the heat and quickly stir in all the nuts. If using, add the salt. Pour immediately onto a lightly oiled baking tray in an even layer. Leave to cool and harden at room temperature for at least 30 minutes. The praline is ready when it is completely solid and snaps cleanly. Break into shards to serve, or transfer to a food processor and grind to your preferred texture.
